straight_beat.name: Reuben Tachibana
straight_beat.column:
The rotation gave up three earned runs. The bullpen coughed up four. The A’s took the series. The move to Sacramento is still pending. Those are the facts.  

Let’s talk about the rotation. Rich Hill started, which is about as predictable as a late-September rainstorm. He pitched five innings, allowed three runs, and struck out six. Not bad for a guy who’s been through the wringer. But here’s the thing: the A’s have been asking their starters to do more than just pitch. They’ve been asking them to be saviors. To carry games when the offense can’t. To make up for the fact that, for the third straight week, the bats have gone dormant.  

Look, I get it. The offense is a work in progress. The ledger says otherwise. But the ledger also says the rotation has been a liability for most of this season. This isn’t a new problem. It’s a recurring one. The Moneyball era taught us that you don’t win championships with a 1-2 punch and a bullpen that leaks like a sieve. You build a team that can score runs and keep them in the game. The A’s have been trying to do both. They’ve been trying to be the team that wins with pitching and wins with offense. The problem is, they’re not doing either well enough.  

The bullpen, meanwhile, is a circus. They’ve given up four runs in three games. That’s not a fluke. It’s a pattern. The closer, the setup man, the middle reliever—they all seem to have the same

optimist.name: Penny Halloran
optimist.column:
The loss stings, sure. The Rangers’ eight-run barrage yesterday wasn’t just a beatdown—it was a reminder that even the most stubborn of us can’t ignore the weight of a 1.0-game deficit in the AL West. But here’s the thing: this team isn’t just a collection of players; it’s a mosaic of potential, and sometimes the brightest pieces are still being shaped.  

Take Soderstrom, for instance. That cut he made yesterday—sharp, precise, the kind of play that makes you lean forward in your chair—wasn’t just a moment. It was a statement. You look at that cut and tell me we’re not two years from playing October baseball, wherever that field happens to be. The kid’s got the tools, the grit, and the instincts of a guy who’s been waiting for this moment his whole life. And when you’ve been waiting for it, you don’t let a single loss define you.  

The front office has been busy, too. They’re not just stacking the roster—they’re building something that can weather the storms. Young arms, savvy veterans, a bullpen that’s still finding its rhythm. You can’t ignore the fact that this team is still in the mix. Eight games over .500? That’s not a fluke. It’s a signboard flashing “Here we go.”  

And let’s not forget the Coliseum. That hallowed ground, that green and gold forever, still pulses with energy. Even on days like yesterday, when the scoreboard reads “Rangers 8, A’s 1,” the fans in the stands are the ones who keep the heartbeat

pessimist.name: Gil Mosbacher
pessimist.column:
The A’s lost again. Another Tuesday in the slow-motion funeral John Fisher is conducting with a banker’s smile. The Rangers didn’t just beat us—they embarrassed us, 8-1, in a game that felt like a dress rehearsal for a midseason collapse. You could taste the rot in the air, the kind that comes from a team that’s been told for years it’s “on the rise” but never actually is. Eight wins, eight losses. A .500 record. A 1.0-game gap in the AL West. It’s not the numbers that sting, though. It’s the way they’ve arrived there—by trading away the future, by letting the present wither, by pretending that a .260 average and a 4.3 ERA are somehow indicators of progress.  

Fisher out. Always Fisher out. The man has a talent for making decisions that look like they were made by a spreadsheet rather than a strategist. Last week, we traded a prospect with a name that rhymes with “hope” for a minor-league reliever who’ll probably never pitch in a major-league game. This week, we’re told “we’re building” and “we’re patient,” but the patience is wearing thin. The A’s aren’t building anything. They’re dismantling a roster that once had the faintest glimmer of potential. Remember when we had a rotation? When we had a bullpen? When we had players who didn’t look like they’d been drafted in 200
